title: Collecting Relevance Judgments
concept: Gathering labeled query-document relevance data from click logs, human annotation, or LLM-assisted labeling.

exercises.prompt: Which metric uses a discount factor to penalize highly relevant documents that are placed lower in the search results?
exercises.explain: NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) is the standard metric that discounts the value of relevant results if they appear lower down in the list.

cards.title: The Fuel for Ranking
cards.body: We know how to calculate ranking scores like NDCG, but where do we get the actual relevance scores for our documents? Without high-quality labels showing which documents match which queries, our ranking algorithms are flying blind.

cards.title: Relevance Judgments
cards.body: Relevance judgments are labels that rate how well a document answers a search query. These labels can be binary (yes/no) or graded (from 0 to 4) and are crucial for training and evaluating ranking models.

cards.title: The Guide Dog Training
cards.body: Think of relevance judgments like training a guide dog. You cannot just tell the dog to 'be good'; you must explicitly reward it when it steers you toward the correct path and correct it when it goes the wrong way.

cards.title: Graded Relevance in Action
cards.body: If a user searches for 'healthy salad recipes', a page with a Caesar salad recipe gets a high relevance score of 3. A page about the history of lettuce gets a 1, and a page about smartphone repairs gets a 0.

cards.title: The Click Trap
cards.body: Many believe that click logs are the perfect source of truth. However, users often click the first result simply because it is at the top, not because it is the most relevant. This is known as position bias.

cards.title: Data Sourcing Tradeoffs
cards.body: Different labeling methods offer distinct tradeoffs between cost, speed, and accuracy.

left.text: Massive volume and low cost, but noisy and biased by placement.

right.text: High quality and nuanced, but slow and very expensive.

exercises.prompt: How do LLM-assisted judges generate relevance labels for a query-document pair?

options.text: They manually click on search results in real-time.
They use prompts to score the match between a query and a document.
They track the user's cursor movements on the screen.
They completely replace the need for ranking algorithms.

exercises.explain: LLMs can evaluate relevance quickly and at low cost by comparing the query text directly against the document text using a prompt.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Human annotation is the cheapest and fastest way to gather millions of relevance judgments.
exercises.explain: Human annotation is highly accurate and captures nuance, but it is slow and expensive to scale compared to automatic click tracking or LLM API calls.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the term that describes why click logs can be misleading due to layout design.
exercises.sentence: Users clicking the top result just because it is first is a classic example of position bias.
exercises.explain: Position bias happens when users click the first few results simply because of where they are placed on the screen, not because they are actually relevant.

exercises.prompt: Identify the relevance judgment generated by an LLM-assisted labeling pipeline.

options.text: User clicked on item #3 after hovering for 4 seconds.
Relevance Score: 3/4. Reason: The document directly answers the query's intent but lacks up-to-date sources.
SELECT document_id FROM search_logs WHERE query = 'best laptop';

exercises.explain: Option B mimics an automated LLM evaluator assigning a structured numerical relevance grade and reasoning, whereas the other options represent raw user interactions or manual database lookups.

exercises.prompt: You are building a search engine for medical records. How should you design your relevance judgment pipeline to balance cost, scale, and accuracy?

options.text: Use only click data because it has zero bias and is completely free of noise.
Rely 100% on LLM judges because they are always perfectly aligned with human preferences.
Use click logs for scale, human reviews for high-stakes gold standards, and LLMs for rapid prototyping.
Avoid using human annotators altogether because they are slower than click logs.

exercises.explain: Implicit signals like clicks are cheap and abundant but noisy (due to position bias). Human labels are accurate but slow and expensive. LLMs as judges offer speed and cheap scaling, but they can be biased or miss subtle domain details. A hybrid system combines the best of all worlds!
